edges.description: Aberrant BMP signaling drives differentiation of muscle and connective tissue progenitor cells into chondrocytes and osteoblasts, leading to formation of qualitatively normal bone at ectopic sites.
Aberrant ACVR1 signaling during embryonic patterning disrupts first digit development.
Mutant receptor acquires abnormal signaling response to Activin A.
Loss of FKBP1A inhibition lowers the activation threshold for mutant ACVR1 signaling.
Injury-related Activin A release aberrantly activates mutant ACVR1 in local tissues.
Activin A-driven mutant receptor signaling initiates ectopic chondrogenic and osteogenic programs.
Bridging ectopic bone progressively ankyloses major joints.
Early periarticular ossification reduces range of motion.
Asymmetric axial heterotopic bone and contractures distort spinal alignment.
Thoracic cage ossification limits chest wall expansion and lung mechanics.
Cytokine-rich flare environments recruit progenitors and promote endochondral ectopic bone formation.

nodes.description: The R206H mutation renders ACVR1 responsive to Activin A ligands, which normally antagonize BMP signaling. This neomorphic gain-of-function allows Activin A to aberrantly activate osteogenic signaling, driving heterotopic ossification. Anti-Activin A antibodies can block HO. Activin A is an obligate factor for the initiation of HO in FOP.

evidence.reference_title: Palovarotene Inhibits Heterotopic Ossification and Maintains Limb Mobility and Growth in Mice With the Human ACVR1(R206H) Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva (FOP) Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Most FOP patients carry an activating mutation in a bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) type I receptor gene, ACVR1(R206H), that promotes ectopic chondrogenesis and osteogenesis and, in turn, HO

evidence.reference_title: Deformity of the great toe in fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
evidence.snippet: A shortened great toe and hallux valgus were frequently found in patients with FOP.

nodes.description: The ACVR1 R206H mutation creates a pH-sensitive switch in the receptor's activation domain, leading to ligand-independent activation of BMP signaling and inappropriate osteogenic differentiation of connective tissue progenitors.

evidence.reference_title: Functional modeling of the ACVR1 (R206H) mutation in FOP.
evidence.snippet: Protein modeling predicts that substitution with histidine, and only histidine, creates a pH-sensitive switch within the activation domain of the receptor that leads to ligand-independent activation of ACVR1 in fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.

evidence.reference_title: Palovarotene Inhibits Heterotopic Ossification and Maintains Limb Mobility and Growth in Mice With the Human ACVR1(R206H) Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva (FOP) Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP), a rare and as yet untreatable genetic disorder of progressive extraskeletal ossification, is the most disabling form of heterotopic ossification (HO) in humans

nodes.description: The R206H mutation reduces binding affinity for FKBP1A/FKBP12, a safeguard protein that normally prevents inappropriate BMP signaling, resulting in leaky activation of the pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular consequences of the ACVR1(R206H) mutation of fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
evidence.snippet: The R206H mutant showed a decreased binding affinity for FKBP1A/FKBP12, a known safeguard molecule against the leakage of transforming growth factor (TGF)-beta or BMP signaling

nodes.description: Macrophages, mast cells, and lymphocytes infiltrate affected tissues during the catabolic phase, releasing inflammatory cytokines that create a permissive microenvironment for heterotopic ossification. Mast cell depletion reduces HO by approximately 50%, and combined mast cell/macrophage depletion reduces HO by approximately 75%.

evidence.reference_title: Palovarotene Inhibits Heterotopic Ossification and Maintains Limb Mobility and Growth in Mice With the Human ACVR1(R206H) Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva (FOP) Mutation.
evidence.snippet: Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP), a rare and as yet untreatable genetic disorder of progressive extraskeletal ossification, is the most disabling form of heterotopic ossification (HO) in humans

evidence.reference_title: Respiratory oscillometry in individuals with fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
evidence.snippet: Spirometry showed a uniform pattern of restrictive physiology in all eight participants with no significant difference amongst the group.

evidence.reference_title: Early mortality and cardiorespiratory failure in patients with fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
evidence.snippet: The most common causes of death in patients with fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva were cardiorespiratory failure from thoracic insufficiency syndrome (54%; median age, forty-two years) and pneumonia (15%; median age, forty years).

evidence.reference_title: Spinal deformity in patients who have fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva.
evidence.snippet: Twenty-six (65 per cent) of the patients had scoliosis, which, according to the clinical records and the recollection of the patients, had been present during childhood.
